items.prompt: A problem that seeks to maximise or minimise a quantity such as profit or cost belongs to the general class called:
items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch12 Sec 12.1 Optimisation problems

items.prompt: The linear function Z = ax + by (with a, b constants) that has to be maximised or minimised is called the:
items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch12 Sec 12.2 Objective function

items.prompt: In the objective function Z = ax + by, the variables x and y are called the:
items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch12 Sec 12.2 Decision variables

items.prompt: The linear inequalities or restrictions on the variables of a linear programming problem are called:
The conditions x >= 0 and y >= 0 imposed on the decision variables are called the:
items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch12 Sec 12.2 Non-negative restrictions

items.prompt: A linear programming problem requires the decision variables to be non-negative and to satisfy a set of:
items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch12 Sec 12.2 Definition of LPP

items.prompt: The common region determined by all the constraints, including the non-negative constraints, of an LPP is called the:
items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch12 Sec 12.2 Feasible region

items.prompt: Points within and on the boundary of the feasible region of an LPP represent:
items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch12 Sec 12.2 Feasible solutions

items.prompt: A point in the feasible region that gives the optimal (maximum or minimum) value of the objective function is called an:
items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch12 Sec 12.2 Optimal solution

items.prompt: A feasible region of a system of linear inequalities is said to be bounded if:
items.answer: it can be enclosed within a circle

expected.correct: it can be enclosed within a circle
options: it can be enclosed within a circle
it extends indefinitely in some direction
it lies in the first quadrant
it has exactly four corner points

items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch12 Sec 12.2 Bounded vs unbounded

items.prompt: A corner point of a feasible region is a point in the region which is the intersection of:

options: the objective function and a constraint
two feasible solutions in the interior

items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch12 Sec 12.2 Corner point (footnote)

items.prompt: By Theorem 1, when the objective function Z has an optimal value, that value must occur at:
items.answer: a corner point (vertex) of the feasible region

expected.correct: a corner point (vertex) of the feasible region
options: a corner point (vertex) of the feasible region
the centre of the feasible region
any interior point of the feasible region

items.prompt: By Theorem 2, if the feasible region R is bounded, then the objective function Z has:
items.answer: both a maximum and a minimum value, each at a corner point

expected.correct: both a maximum and a minimum value, each at a corner point
options: both a maximum and a minimum value, each at a corner point

items.prompt: When two corner points give the same maximum value of Z, which other points also give that maximum value?
items.answer: every point on the line segment joining the two corner points

expected.correct: every point on the line segment joining the two corner points
options: every point on the line segment joining the two corner points
only the two corner points themselves
every point in the whole feasible region

items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch12 Example 3 Remark (multiple optimal solutions)

items.prompt: Maximise Z = 3x + 2y + 1 subject to x ≥ 0, y ≥ 0, 3x + 4y ≤ 12, and one missing constraint. From the graph of the feasible region, a student sees a boundary line through (1, 0) and (0, 2) with the origin excluded from the feasible region. What is the missing constraint? (A) x + 2y ≤ 2  (B) 2x + y ≥ 2  (C) 2x + y ≤ 2  (D) x + 2y ≥ 2
items.quote: B) 2x + y ≥ 2. The points (1,0) and (0,2) satisfy 2x + y = 2; origin excluded ⇒ 2x + y ≥ 2.

items.prompt: In Example 1, Z = 4x + y is evaluated at the corner points (0,0), (30,0), (20,30), (0,50). The maximum value of Z is 120; at which corner point does it occur (give the x-coordinate)?
By the Corner Point Method, to find the optimum of an LPP you evaluate the objective function Z = ax + by at:
items.answer: each corner point of the feasible region

expected.correct: each corner point of the feasible region
options: each corner point of the feasible region
the centre of the feasible region
every interior point of the feasible region

items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch12 Sec 12.2.2 Corner Point Method
